title: Layer Normalization
summary: Normalize across $d_{\text{model}}$ per token, reparameterize with learned $\gamma$ (scale) and $\beta$ (shift). Each block's LN has separate $\gamma/\beta$ because it sees different accumulated residual stream statistics.
key_insights: Two phases: normalize (subtract mean, divide by std) then reparameterize (scale by $\gamma$, shift by $\beta$)
$\gamma, \beta \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}}}$ — per dimension, shared across all tokens
NOT redundant with embeddings: by block $N$, input is $X_0 + \sum \Delta_i$ — no single param controls this
Pre-LN (modern): normalize BEFORE sub-layer. Post-LN (original): after. Pre-LN more stable.

derivations.title: Jacobian of LayerNorm — how gradients flow through normalization

steps.name: Interpretation
steps.math: $J_{\text{LN}} \approx \frac{1}{\sigma} I$ for large $d$ (rank-1 terms become small relative to $I$). Gradient directions approximately preserved, magnitudes rescaled by $1/\sigma$.

derivations.consequence: LN preserves gradient directions while rescaling magnitudes. Healthy gradient flow as long as $\sigma$ doesn't become very small.

derivations.title: Why $\gamma/\beta$ are not redundant — formal argument

steps.name: At block 1
steps.math: Input $= X_0 = W_{\text{embed}}[\text{tokens}] + \text{PE}$. LN could be absorbed into $W_{\text{embed}}$. So at block 1, $\gamma/\beta$ ARE nearly redundant.

steps.name: At block $k > 1$
steps.math: Input $= X_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k-1}(\Delta_{\text{attn}_i} + \Delta_{\text{FFN}_i})$. Sum of $2(k-1)+1$ terms from different weight matrices. No single matrix controls the statistics.

steps.name: What $\gamma/\beta$ learn per block
steps.math: $\gamma_k$: relative importance of dimension $j$ at depth $k$. $\beta_k$: optimal bias at depth $k$. Block-specific because residual stream statistics evolve.

derivations.consequence: Redundancy decreases with depth. $\gamma/\beta$ most useful in deeper blocks where accumulated residual stream is furthest from any single parameter's control.

derivations.title: Pre-LN vs Post-LN gradient flow

steps.name: Post-LN (original)
steps.math: $X_{\text{out}} = \text{LN}(X + \text{SubLayer}(X))$. Gradient passes through $J_{\text{LN}}$, which can distort the identity shortcut.

steps.name: Pre-LN (modern)
steps.math: $X_{\text{out}} = X + \text{SubLayer}(\text{LN}(X))$. Identity $I$ is OUTSIDE $J_{\text{LN}}$ — gradient always has clean path.

derivations.consequence: Pre-LN preserves the residual gradient highway. Post-LN can distort it. Pre-LN trains more stably in deep networks.

geometric_properties: LN projects onto hyperplane (subtract mean) and normalizes length (divide by std)
Post-norm vectors lie near a $(d_{\text{model}}-1)$-dimensional sphere
statistical_properties: After LN: mean $\approx \beta$, std $\approx |\gamma|$ per dimension
Monitor learned $\gamma$: collapse toward 0 = layer suppressed
Monitor pre-norm mean/variance across layers: should be stable
numerical_properties: $\epsilon = 10^{-5}$ prevents division by zero. float16: use $\epsilon = 10^{-3}$ or compute LN in float32
NaN/Inf post-norm almost always = LN numerical issue in mixed precision

related: TRANSFORMER.RESIDUALS — LN stabilizes what residuals accumulate
Batch normalization — normalizes across batch dimension instead

discussion_history: Explored non-redundancy via residual stream argument
